sentences.text: Yes, well, I was-- as I mentioned, very early I had a very pessimistic point of view.
And I, I, I saw no sense and no reason for self-delusion as I witnessed all around me among the adult population.

And I have decided that, in wartime, when conflagration is starting and just reaching you, the only thing to do is if you-- and you can't stand and fight anyhow, so you run away, especially if you've got the sort of an enemy that is-- with the Russians, you certainly couldn't.
You didn't fight a, a, a actual war.
You fought a political war.
You fought a, an economic--
--ideological war, but not, not a,a war of-- actually.
So I have decided to go.
And I went.
I crossed the border to Wilno because Wilno [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] going to be taken over by the Lithuanians at the behest of the Russians.
Having occupied-- the Russians having occupied Wilno in their march westwards to meet the Germans, they were very generous to the Lithuanians.
They turned out [INAUDIBLE] the Lithuanians and said, Wilno is the nominal capital of Lithuania in your constitution-- since the First World War, occupied by the Poles.
So to do the right thing by you, we will return Wilno to you.
And you can bring your capital of Lithuania from the little town of Kovno to its rightful place, Wilno.
And, and that's when the gratitude and the recognition of the Lithuanian population for the gesture of magnanimity.
Obviously, what they did is they've returned Wilno to the Lithuanians and then swallowed the whole of Lithuania.
Did you need special papers to leave Bialystok?
No, I went-- I went illegally.
I went across the border illegally.
No.
The only papers I had was my, my personal papers that I took with me, the school papers, and the-- and the-- and the university papers, and the-- whatever-- I can't, I can't say exactly what.
And my father gave myself an amount of money, cash, to carry with me.
And I went because-- the nature of running away is that you have got very little time to think.
All you know is that you want to run.
And the biblical story about Lot's wife, that as she looked backwards and she was turned in salt-- into salt is a, a human story of saying to people, if you run away from something, don't look back.
The minute you look back, then you will never run away, then you will never cut the bridges.
You will never burn the bridges behind you.
Yes.
Some of my schoolmates.
Classmates from school went with me.
One of them we carried-- we carried literally on ourselves because he couldn't-- we walked in snow right up to here [POINTS] across the border.
And he couldn't-- he was a weakling, not that I was such a strong fellow myself, but by comparison with him.
We walked a whole night.
The whole night.
We, we, we left about 12 o'clock at night.
And we arrived there at 7:00 in the morning.
Well, I can describe to you what happened on the way.
We have nearly lost ourselves there because it started to rain in the middle of the night.
And we-- and we saw an unfinished building on our way.
And we went in there for shelter.
But much to our misfortune, the border guards who were-- it was right on the border-- also wanted to, to shelter themselves from the rain.
And they also went in, into the same, unfinished building, just sheltering from the rain.
We saw them coming.
And we hugged the wall in the dark.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they came in.
And they struck a match to, to, to, light a cigarette.
And at that second, the place was lit up.
But they didn't notice us.
We laid there, breathless, I don't know for how long, until they smoked their cigarette and they left.
And we waited for a good while.
But there, there was a dog barking in the distance, and so on.
And when we-- when the-- when the leader of ours-- the, the man who was leading us across the border, felt that it is reasonably safe, we, we continued on the journey.
And we made it.
We made it.
We came 7 o'clock in the morning to Wilno.
And we hired somewhere a, a, a, a cart and horse to take us to, to town.
Well, we did-- I had a very particular situation.
We had an agent in Wilno.
So I went to the agent's home.
And for a little while, I stayed there.
I paid them board and rent.
They were poor people themselves.
So, but after a while, there was a major, major influx, the same as was the situation in Bialystok first.
It was even bigger because it was accelerated, accumulating.
And the wave of people accumulated running now away from Poland altogether came to Wilno.
It was about three, three-- it was-- one on the northern part was in Wilno.
There was one on the center and then one in the-- in Lemberg in, in, in the-- in the-- in the south.
And we had to organize our lives there.
And there, almost overnight, clubs were organized for the-- for the people to have somewhere to come, or where to share, share social contact and to get news, and to discuss things, and so on.
And we had-- we took-- we took premises.
And we organized clubs.
And some of the people who played later on a very important part in the-- in the-- people who-- leaders of the-- of the Polish Jewry were part-- members of this club.
And later on, members of these created the Israeli government-- Kleinbaum, Moshe Kleinbaum, who was, who was later Moshe Sneh.
The first Israeli government was, was, was with us in that-- at that club.

And he was the man who went, who went to, to interfere-- to intervene with the Russian ambassador in Kovno because there were a number of people who had Palestinian certificates but couldn't go the rest of the way.
The only way for them to go was via Odessa, the south.
For that, you needed a transit visa from Russia.
And it was decided to attempt to persuade the Russian authorities to let us have a transit visa to Russia.
And Kleinbaum went with the editor of the of Kovno, who knew the ambassador, for an audience there, made an application.
And lo and behold, the people got transit visas.
And they escaped via Odessa.
And they got to Palestine in time.
That-- so that was part, part of the-- food was, was not provided there.
People had to, to manage the best way they could on an individual basis.
In fact, for a while, we lived a very, very charming life.
It was-- it was not yet a big upheaval.
And life in Wilno was still going on normally.
They did-- the restaurants were open.
The music was playing for lunch, you know?

The-- as the saying goes, 'The house burns, and the-- and the clock ticks.' And so the absurdities, the contradictions were everywhere, everywhere, everywhere, in terms of, of social behavior and interactions, and so on, as if nothing has happened, as if nothing-- and yet, the, the, the ground was removed from, from everybody's-- under their feet.
But, but the behavioral patterns were so ingrained and so, and so kept to, to give some sort of semblance of normality of maybe a, a, a, a, a form of self-assurance that you are still a human being.
There is still some sort of a dignity left in your personality, and so on, hoping that maybe, maybe day after tomorrow may not be as tragic and as-- as it turned out to be.
A year.
I stayed there a year.
But here comes into play the famous historical fact that turned out to be a savior for a few thousand people.
It was a case of a Dutch rabbinical student who studied at a yeshiva in Poland.
And he was caught in the war.
And he ran, together with his entire yeshiva.
There was the [INAUDIBLE] Yeshiva-- 400 rabbinical students ran.
The entire yeshiva, with their rabbis, with their managers, with their-- the entire thing went to Wilno.
And this man-- who was not a Polish citizen; he was a Dutch citizen-- wanted to find a way how to get to Holland.
Of course, the western way, you couldn't because by that time, France and Holland was occupied, and so on.
He went-- he went to see the Dutch consulate.
And the Dutch consulate gave him a visa to Curacao.
And Curacao was a, a, a, a Dutch protectorate.
To get there, he had to go via Russia and Japan to get to Curacao.
And this man, on, on a single basis, obtained a transit visa via Russia and obtained a transit visa from the Japanese consul in Kovno.
And when he came with these papers and documents stamped [NON-ENGLISH] to go, the, the-- these, these rabbinical students saw a ray of light.
If he can go out that way, why can't we?
All right, we are not Dutch citizens.
But we will go as far as we can.
We will-- we will go via Russia.
We will go via Japan.
And there, maybe we can get to America.
Because by that time, the, the American Jewry, and particularly, the rabbinical movement in America, was aware about the plight of this particular yeshiva.
And they wanted to save them en masse to take care of the whole thing.

So that is how the, the fantastic story of the Japanese consul in Kovno, who decided, on his own accord without consulting the foreign office, to issue 10-day transit visas via Japan for people who bought, ostensibly, visas to Curacao, which were equally worthless.
Because Curacao had total immigration restrictions.
Nobody could get into Curacao.
And he knew it.
But it was a sufficient formality, on the basis of such an end visa, to obtain a transit visa for 10 days.
And that's-- and there's a book written about the Fugu Plan-- you may have heard the name about.
And that is how 2,000 Polish Jews from-- in Wilno have, have managed to obtain such transit visas-- such end visas to Curacao and to obtain a visa via Japan.
But then there was one little other thing.
You had to get from Wilno to Vladivostok, the Trans-Siberian Railways.
For that, you needed a Russian transit visa.
And that was a horse of a different caliber.
To ask to go-- to get out of Russia was tantamount of saying, we don't want to live under the communist regime.
It was treason, in terms of their ideological understanding.
And yet, there was so little to lose that some people decided they will take a chance.
What can it be?
We knew that they played a trick in Lemberg.

As soon as they moved in there, at the end of, in the end of September, beginning of October, they put a notice in the paper of the travel agency, of the official travel agency, that everybody who has got [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] relatives overseas and wants to join them should register at the Orbis office.
And people, in their innocence, queued for days and registered.
And then they took all those lists and called the people in.
And they said to them, it's not good enough for you here under our regime, is it?
All right, we will send you somewhere else.
But we won't send you where you want to go.
You will go to, to Siberia.
And they packed them up and sent them to Siberia, all of them.
And we knew it.
And yet, we have taken-- some of us were brave enough to take the first steps and say, we'll take a chance.
Because we, we also knew that the people who had the certificates in, in, in Wilno got transit and went to Odessa.
So it was not altogether an impossibility.
We had a precedent where certain transit visas were obtained.
I was one of the first to apply, one of the very first to apply.
And we waited.
And we were preparing ourselves simultaneously for two other eventualities.
Either we will get out or will go to Siberia.
Both-- the Siberian eventuality was much more realistic than the other one.
And lo and behold, one day, the first notices came out on the wall of the [NON-ENGLISH], nowhere less but the Secret Service.
The first list, I came, and I looked.
There was nothing there.
The second list, a few days later, I looked.
I couldn't believe my eyes. 'Israel Kipen' was on that list.
And I got my transit visa.
And we offered to pay in American dollars for the pleasure of traveling the 12 days and 12 nights Trans-Siberian.
And that's how we got out to Japan.
I, myself, at a later stage, became a Dutch consul.
I had a facsimile of the-- of the Dutch visa.
All we used to do is cover, cover my name and put a name of somebody else on top.
And that was how I gave other people Dutch visas to Curacao on the basis of which they went to get Japanese visas and transit, and so on.
I've still got the original document.
And it is incorporated in my book.
The journey took 12 days and 12 nights nonstop, with a stop in Moscow for three days.
We stopped-- we came from Wilno via means into Moscow.
There, we stayed in a posh hotel for three days.
And then we took the train eastwards, which took 12 days and 12 nights.
Today it takes five days.
No, no.

It so happened that there was a, a, a family of Bialystok people who was a, a renowned manufacturer, who, when he arrived in Shanghai-- in, in, in Shanghai, ultimately-- first in Japan, then in Shanghai-- was in the extraordinary situation that they have sent out goods just before the war to Harbin.
And he was in a position to collect the money for the goods that they have sent out there.
And he was an exception.
But otherwise, we have parted with whatever we had.
I certainly parted whatever we had to pay for the ticket in American dollars.
By the time I, I arrived in Japan, I had $10 on me.
That was the sum total of my wealth and worldly possessions.
No.
It was-- it was-- in terms of, of the, as I said before, it was not a, a, a, a, a problem for the Jews.
It was a problem for the strata of the population.
Wilno was a poor city.
It was a big city.
It was a city of culture, of Yiddish writers, of YIVO.
The YIVO Institute, its origin was in, in, in, in Wilno.
But it was a poor city.
You saw-- you saw beggars in the streets.
It was a city which was cut out like the York peninsula on the Australian map.
They, they occupied Wilno.
But they didn't occupy the surroundings of Wilno.
So it had to leave from the surroundings of the south because the, the north and east and the west were Lithuanian territory, which, which was not under Polish suzerainty.

And therefore, even in terms of, of the cultural basis for existence, they were cut off like, like, like Vienna was after the-- after the First World War when the-- when the-- when the, the Hapsburg Empire fell, fell to pieces and the Hinterland was completely taken away from them.
And they were just a big city like a white elephant.
And that was the Wilno situation.
But, but in terms of, of, you know, the Jewish situation was only-- it affected the, the, the upper classes, in terms of the value system of the government, of the Russian government.
But it did not affect the people as Jews, as Jews.
Yes, I have managed to, to communicate with my-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with my uncle.
But it was an extraordinary thing.
The neighbor-- a young man, a neighbor in our house-- also ran away.
He was an only son.
He ran way to Wilno.
And I met him in Wilno.
And he told me the story, the extraordinary story, what happened to my family.
Because my father finally decided to leave Poland, but again, with the same illogicality in which he behaved first.
And I don't say that with disrespect.
He made a decision and left his mother and his family behind and crossed the border on his own.
And he was caught.
He was caught on the border, which wasn't extraordinary.
And he was jailed.
And when he was interrogated, he had to tell them who he was.
And by the nature of the fact that he had such a great enterprise, having a few knitting machines in his best room in the house, he was a capitalist.
He was jailed for three years for crossing the border and for his capitalist past.
And he had to give the address of his home.
And one morning, the-- a military truck arrived at 19 [POLISH] Street and gave my mother and the children half an hour to pack.
And they were put on the truck and taken away.
My grandmother fainted, being an elderly woman by then.
And they thought that she's dead.
So they left her.
And they were taken away to Siberia.
They were sent to Siberia.
They finished up in Pavlodar.
And that's how they saved themselves.
But father managed, from prison, to get a exchange of letters with his brother, who told him what happened to my mother and the children, and to give him mother's address in Kazakhstan.
He had mother's address in Kazakhstan.

And when, when he was ultimately freed from jail, he made, on his own, the journey all the way-- you know, at one stage, when I went on the Trans-Siberian Railway, I was a mere 600 kilometers from where my mother was, a mere 600 kilometers away.
I couldn't even whisper about it to anybody.
And my brother told me the story that while they were working within their jobs-- and one morning they went by foot.
They had to go by foot.
They had many miles to go to work.
One morning, my brother went to work in the hays of a field, and so on.
Out of the distance, he saw a figure swaying towards him.
And the boat moved in the opposite direction, of course, until he looked and said, my god, that looks like my father.
And so it was.
He came after three years, three years in a Russian jail.
And in jail, he refused to eat the food because it wasn't kosher.
And even at Pesach time, he refused to have the little bit of bread that was given them daily because he wouldn't eat bread on Pesach.

So-- and there are stories being-- that, that were told to me here about how his endurance in jail gave all the people there a, a feeling of strength to, to endure what they were enduring because of the-- of the inner strength of these men.
The, the people-- the jailers pleaded with him that he should eat because they said, you will die.
You won't survive it.
You want survive it.
Nothing doing.
He used to, at Pesach time, exchange his piece of bread with somebody's bit of potatoes.
So he ate the potatoes, which was possible.
But he wouldn't eat the bread.
He came out there, it was skin and bones, of course.
But he survived.
And that is how, how they-- they survived the war.
And that is-- and I was, from Shanghai afterwards, corresponding with mother and father.
It took nine months for such a letter to make the around-the-world trip, [INAUDIBLE].
And I used to write to them in Russian so that the Russian censor could read it.
Otherwise, it would have never got to them.
My Russian was somewhat less than perfect.
But they most probably had a good chuckles in reading it, and so on.
But I, I did it.
And I used to get replies.
And I've got some reply cards from them, yellow, brownish open cards from them in Russian of, of those years-- I've got in my possession.
Of course we knew about the collapse of France and the low countries.
We knew that.
And to the extent to which it was at all possible, we knew.
And then, of course, by the time we came to Shanghai and Pearl Harbor came into being, we knew all about it because we were trapped there for the duration.
Yes, we had just, we just arrived.
We were in Japan-- instead of 10 days, we were 10 months.
And, and, and, and were it not for the decision to go to war, they would have kept us probably a lot longer.
But they didn't want to feed us for the duration of the war.
We were alien, yes?
If they-- if they go to war, they would have had to keep us in concentration camps and feed us.
So they have sent us out to Shanghai, which was an open city.
They didn't need visas for Shanghai.
So they put us on a boat and dumped us in Shanghai.
And they knew very well that, in a couple of months time, they will be there, too, anyhow.
What date did you arrive in Japan?
In Japan-- I arrived in February, 1941.
We arrived in the-- in Suruga, the port of Suruga, which was, of course, the straits from Vladivostok.
And it was a journey overnight where we almost paid with our lives.
We traveled in a cattle boat.
And it's one of the worst crossings in the world.
We were sick of it.
OK.
I got it.
